runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 2 in a U.S. microsimulation model under current-law tax-benefit rules.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) review of taxable-income elasticities
Blundell and Hoynes work on labor supply responses in tax-benefit settings
Diamond and Saez optimal income taxation framework
runs.reasoningSummary: I expect low-wage primary earners to have somewhat positive but modest substitution responsiveness in a current-law microsimulation, with decile 2 likely below prime-age/upper-income elasticities. The distribution is skewed right because some low-income workers can be much more responsive, but many are constrained by hours, job frictions, or eligibility rules.
